ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mee Goreng; 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03311069) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Mee Goreng entry; A0CKP (stir-fried noodles) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Mee Goreng or a sufficiently close stir-fried noodle dish concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small serving of Mee Goreng is approximately 300 grams, suitable for one person as a light meal or as part of a shared spread for 1–3 people.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Goreng (Malay Fried Noodles) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo reports Mee Goreng per 100 g cooked weight. Values are adapted from the HPB Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food database entry for Mee Goreng (Malay-style stir-fried yellow noodles). The restaurant version from The Seafood Market may have higher sodium and fat due to commercial preparation; values represent a best-available reference for this dish category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; yellow wheat noodles, cooked, analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mee Goreng. GI of 49 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked yellow wheat noodles / Asian-style stir-fried noodles (GI range 40–55, representative value 49). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (49 × 64.5 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 31.6, rounded to 31.9 accounting for sauce sugars. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1440 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Mee Goreng to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce, as a single small serving can contribute approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined yellow wheat noodles and added sugars in sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Mee Goreng in controlled portions (150–200 g) and pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glycemic response, given the moderate glycemic load of approximately 32 per standard small serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Mee Goreng should be consumed sparingly due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less oil during preparation can help reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Goreng (马来炒面, Malay Fried Noodles) is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian stir-fried noodle dish made with yellow wheat noodles cooked in a hot wok with egg, tofu, potato, tomato, bean sprouts, green chilli, soy sauce, and chilli sauce. The dish is characterised by its vibrant orange-red colour, bold spicy-savoury flavour, and slightly chewy noodle texture. It is a staple of Malay hawker stalls and coffeeshops across Singapore and is commonly served as a single-portion meal or shared dish. Nutritionally, a 300 g small serving provides approximately 444 kcal, 17 g protein, 13.5 g fat, and 64.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~1440 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 49 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~32), relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is generally halal and dairy-free but contains gluten (wheat noodles), egg, and soy allergens. Restaurant versions, such as those served at The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon), may incorporate seafood such as crab or prawns, increasing protein content and overall caloric density.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mee goreng?
What noodles are used in mee goreng?
Is mee goreng suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of mee goreng?
Mee goreng vs mee rebus difference
Can I eat mee goreng if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mee Goreng (Malay-style), which is the closest approved primary source for this Singaporean dish. The restaurant version from The Seafood Market may differ in exact composition (e.g., seafood additions, oil quantity, sauce formulation), so confidence is medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked yellow wheat noodles.
notes: 1. Vitamin B6, B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mee Goreng. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source for this dish category). 3. The input record describes this as '(S) MEE GORENG 马来炒面 (小）' — the '(S)' and '(小）' denote a small serving size, preserved in the food_name and serving fields. 4. The restaurant (The Seafood Market by House of White Bee Hoon) specialises in seafood; their Mee Goreng may include crab or prawns not reflected in the base nutrient profile. 5. GI of 49 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked yellow wheat noodles; no direct GI measurement for Mee Goreng was found in the approved GI database. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 6. The cuisine tag in the input lists 'Chinese' but this dish is Malay in origin and preparation style; cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean Malay'. 7. All -1 sentinel values represent micronutrients not reported in the HPB source database for this dish.
